cards.title: What this screening does
cards.body: CogniTrace is a voice screening tool. It analyzes a recorded voice sample with a classical ONNX ensemble to look for patterns that may be worth monitoring or discussing with a clinician, but it does not diagnose Parkinson's disease.

cards.title: What screening means
cards.body: A screening result is an early signal, not a medical conclusion. It can help flag whether a voice pattern looks more or less concerning compared with the model's training data, but it cannot confirm disease, rule disease out completely, or replace a clinical evaluation.

cards.title: Why voice can be studied in Parkinson-related research
cards.body: Voice is sometimes studied because changes in vocal control, loudness stability, timing, and sound quality can occur in neurological and non-neurological conditions. A voice sample can therefore provide useful screening information, but voice alone is not enough to make a diagnosis.

cards.title: What the prediction model is and what Gemma is used for
cards.body: The predictive score comes from the classical ONNX ensemble. Gemma is used only for plain-language summary, grounded follow-up education, and doctor discussion guide generation, not for making the screening prediction from raw audio.

cards.title: Why one recording should be interpreted carefully
cards.body: A single recording is only one snapshot. Voice can vary from day to day and even within the same day depending on fatigue, hydration, illness, effort, stress, and recording conditions, so one result should be interpreted in context rather than treated as a final answer.

cards.title: Why recording conditions matter
cards.body: Voice measurements work best when the recording is clear and the task is performed consistently. Noise, microphone placement, clipping, interruptions, or an incomplete task can change the measured pattern and make interpretation less reliable.

cards.title: What a low result means
cards.body: A low result means this recording did not strongly resemble the Parkinson-related voice patterns represented in the model's training data. It can be reassuring, but it does not rule out symptoms, future change, or other reasons to seek medical advice.

cards.title: How to think about a low result
cards.body: A low result is usually best read as not strongly concerning from this recording under these conditions. If you still notice voice, speech, movement, or other symptoms, those symptoms matter more than the app alone and are still worth discussing with a clinician.

cards.title: What a moderate result means
cards.body: A moderate result means the screening found some voice patterns worth paying attention to, but not a strong enough signal to stand alone as a severe finding. It is best understood as a prompt to consider context, repeat conditions, and whether there are symptoms or changes over time worth discussing.

cards.title: When a moderate result may deserve extra attention
cards.body: A moderate result may deserve extra attention when it happens more than once, when the recording was made under good conditions, or when the user has noticed voice, speech, movement, or swallowing changes. The result should still be framed as screening information rather than proof of a condition.

cards.title: What an elevated result means
cards.body: An elevated result means the full screening model found a stronger similarity to Parkinson-related voice patterns in its training data. It is a screening signal, not a diagnosis, and should be treated as something to discuss with a clinician rather than proof that a disease is present.

cards.title: How to react to an elevated result
cards.body: An elevated result should be taken seriously enough to review, but not as an emergency claim made by the app. A balanced response is to note the result, remember that confounders can matter, and bring it into a clinician conversation together with symptoms and context.

cards.title: What 'worth discussing with a clinician' means
cards.body: This phrase means the result may add useful information to a medical conversation, especially if you also have symptoms, recent change, or repeated concerning screenings. It does not mean the app has established a diagnosis or that a clinician will necessarily agree with the screening impression.

cards.title: Why repeated results can be more informative than one result
cards.body: Repeated results under similar, good-quality conditions can help show whether a finding seems stable or was likely affected by circumstances. Even then, repeated screenings still do not diagnose disease, and the pattern should be interpreted together with symptoms and clinician judgment.

cards.title: Common things that can affect a recording
cards.body: Fatigue, dehydration, respiratory illness, allergies, hoarseness, stress, microphone distance, background noise, inconsistent effort, and a rushed task can all change voice measurements. These factors can affect the result without reflecting Parkinson-specific change.

cards.title: Fatigue and tiredness
cards.body: Being tired can affect breath support, loudness control, steadiness, and task consistency. That means fatigue can shift voice measurements and should be considered when interpreting a result, especially if the recording was made late in the day or during poor sleep.

cards.title: Dehydration and dry throat
cards.body: Dehydration or throat dryness can make the voice sound rougher or less steady and can alter some acoustic measurements. A dry voice on one day does not by itself mean a neurological problem, so hydration status is useful context when reviewing the result.

cards.title: Cold, cough, allergies, or hoarseness
cards.body: A cold, cough, allergies, reflux irritation, or temporary hoarseness can change pitch stability, loudness stability, and noise-related measures. Results recorded during an active voice problem should be interpreted cautiously because they may reflect temporary illness more than the underlying voice pattern.

cards.title: Background noise
cards.body: Background sound can interfere with measurements that depend on a clean estimate of pitch, energy, and the voice spectrum. Even moderate household noise can reduce reliability, so a quiet room helps make the screening more interpretable.

cards.title: Microphone distance and phone handling
cards.body: Holding the phone too far away, too close, or changing distance during the task can affect loudness and spectrum-related measures. Consistent phone placement helps the app compare the recording more fairly with the patterns it was trained on.

cards.title: Rushed, interrupted, or incomplete task performance
cards.body: If the speaking task was rushed, interrupted, clipped at the start or end, or performed with inconsistent effort, the extracted features may reflect task quality more than the intended voice sample. That is one reason a single result should not be overinterpreted.

cards.title: Stress, anxiety, or unusual effort
cards.body: Stress and unusual speaking effort can change breath support, pitch behavior, and vocal tension. A voice recorded when someone is anxious, straining, or trying hard to sound a certain way may not reflect their usual speaking pattern.

cards.title: Other voice or throat conditions can matter
cards.body: Voice changes can also come from many non-neurological causes such as vocal strain, benign voice disorders, recent intubation, reflux irritation, smoking effects, or upper airway illness. The screening cannot sort out all possible causes from one recording alone.

cards.title: How to think about feature values
cards.body: Feature values are measurements that describe parts of the voice sample, such as steadiness, loudness behavior, and sound quality. They can help characterize the recording, but a single value should not be treated as the sole reason for the screening result.

cards.title: Jitter family
cards.body: Jitter measures short-term pitch instability from one vibration cycle to the next. Higher jitter can reflect less steady vocal control, but it can also be influenced by recording noise, hoarseness, temporary voice strain, and how cleanly the pitch was captured.

cards.title: Why there are several jitter measures
cards.body: Different jitter variants summarize pitch instability over slightly different time windows or averaging rules. They are related measurements rather than separate diagnoses, and they are best understood as technical ways of describing steadiness in the recording.

cards.title: Shimmer family
cards.body: Shimmer measures short-term loudness instability from cycle to cycle. It can reflect less stable vocal fold vibration, but it is also sensitive to breathiness, effort, temporary irritation, microphone behavior, and recording conditions.

cards.title: Why there are several shimmer measures
cards.body: Different shimmer variants summarize loudness instability in slightly different ways. They are technical descriptors used by the model and are usually more useful as part of the full voice profile than as standalone patient-facing conclusions.

cards.title: Harmonics-to-noise ratio
cards.body: Harmonics-to-noise ratio, often shortened to HNR, compares the more periodic part of the voice to noisier components. In general, it helps describe voice quality, but it can change for many reasons and should not be interpreted as proof of one specific disease process.

cards.title: Cepstral peak prominence
cards.body: Cepstral peak prominence, often called CPP, is another technical voice-quality measure related to how strongly periodic the voice signal appears. It can be useful in voice analysis, but it is still one part of a broader pattern rather than a standalone explanation of the screening outcome.

cards.title: Pitch-related measures
cards.body: Pitch-related measures describe average pitch, pitch range, and how much the pitch changes across the recording. They help describe vocal control and speaking pattern, but they are influenced by sex, age, speaking style, task performance, and recording quality, so they are not interpreted in isolation.

cards.title: Average pitch
cards.body: Average pitch, often shown as mean F0, is the typical pitch level across the sample. It can help describe the recording, but average pitch varies naturally between people and across speaking tasks, so it is not meaningful as a diagnosis by itself.

cards.title: Pitch variability
cards.body: Pitch variability measures how much the pitch moves during the sample. Too little or too much variation can both be affected by task style, effort, emotion, and recording quality, so these values are descriptive rather than diagnostic.

cards.title: Pitch range and percentiles
cards.body: Pitch range and pitch percentiles summarize the spread of pitch values in the recording. They provide context about how the voice moved during the task, but they depend strongly on task performance and are not usually interpreted as a single patient-facing biomarker.

cards.title: Voiced fraction
cards.body: Voiced fraction estimates how much of the recording contained stable voiced sound. It can be affected by pauses, breathiness, articulation style, background noise, or incomplete task performance, so it should be treated as context rather than a standalone conclusion.

cards.title: Energy measures
cards.body: Energy measures reflect loudness and vocal drive in the recording. They can be relevant to voice assessment, but they are especially sensitive to microphone distance, phone orientation, speaking effort, and the acoustics of the room.

cards.title: Average versus peak loudness measures
cards.body: Some energy features summarize average loudness, while others capture peak levels. Differences between them can reflect speaking style and recording setup, so these measures are useful descriptors but are not enough on their own to explain the screening output.

cards.title: Spectral measures
cards.body: Spectral measures describe how sound energy is distributed across frequencies. They can help represent voice quality and articulation patterns, but they are less intuitive than classical voice measures and are usually best understood as technical context within the larger model.

cards.title: Spectral centroid
cards.body: Spectral centroid is a technical summary of where the sound energy tends to sit across frequencies. In plain terms, it can relate to how bright or concentrated the sound is, but it is not a patient-friendly standalone marker and is best treated as part of the overall acoustic pattern.

cards.title: Spectral bandwidth
cards.body: Spectral bandwidth describes how spread out the sound energy is across frequencies. It can shift with voice quality, articulation, room acoustics, and noise, so it is useful context for the model but not something that should be overinterpreted by itself.

cards.title: Spectral rolloff and slope
cards.body: Spectral rolloff and spectral slope are technical ways of summarizing how the higher-frequency part of the sound behaves. They can be useful in machine-learning models, but they are not typically translated into simple patient language as isolated biomarkers.

cards.title: Zero-crossing rate
cards.body: Zero-crossing rate is a technical feature that counts how often the waveform changes sign. It can reflect aspects of noisiness or sound texture, but it is not commonly interpreted on its own in patient discussions and is mainly part of the model's broader input set.

cards.title: MFCC features
cards.body: MFCCs are statistical descriptors of the sound spectrum that help summarize the shape of the voice signal. They are useful model inputs, but they are not usually interpreted one by one in patient language and should not be treated as standalone reasons for the result.

cards.title: MFCC averages versus variability
cards.body: For each MFCC coefficient, the model may use both an average value and a variability value across the recording. These are technical summaries of sound shape over time and are generally more useful to the model than they are as direct patient-facing explanations.

cards.title: The app is not a diagnostic test
cards.body: This app is designed for screening and monitoring support, not for diagnosis. A clinician can consider the result together with symptoms, history, examination, and other possible causes, which the app cannot fully capture from voice alone.

cards.title: Why a low result does not fully rule things out
cards.body: A low result means the recording was not strongly concerning to the model under those conditions. It does not guarantee that symptoms are absent, that future change will not occur, or that another type of evaluation would also be low-risk.

cards.title: Single-dataset limitation
cards.body: The predictive model was trained on one research dataset collected under controlled conditions. That means generalization to other populations, other accents, different phones, and noisier real-world settings is limited and should be considered when interpreting results.

cards.title: Controlled recordings differ from home recordings
cards.body: Research recordings are often cleaner and more standardized than everyday home recordings. Differences in room acoustics, microphone handling, and task performance can therefore make real-world results harder to compare directly with the training data.

cards.title: Why one biomarker does not explain the result
cards.body: The screening model used the full feature set together rather than one single measurement. Individual biomarkers can be described and displayed, but they should not be presented as the sole reason the model produced the final screening result.

cards.title: What the app can say about feature values
cards.body: The app can explain what a feature measures in general terms and can show the measured value from your recording. It should not claim that one feature caused the result, because the model used many features together and voice measurements are context-sensitive.

cards.title: The screening does not identify the cause of a voice change
cards.body: Even when a recording looks concerning, the app cannot determine why the voice pattern looks that way. Voice changes may have neurological and non-neurological causes, and sorting those possibilities out requires clinical context beyond the recording alone.

cards.title: Natural differences between people affect voice
cards.body: Voice differs naturally by age, sex, language background, accent, anatomy, and speaking style. The model tries to learn useful patterns from data, but these natural differences are one reason that screening outputs should be interpreted conservatively.

cards.title: Symptoms and clinical context still matter most
cards.body: A screening result is only one data point. If symptoms, recent changes, or family concerns are present, those concerns still matter regardless of whether the result was low, moderate, or elevated, and they can be important to share with a clinician.

cards.title: The app does not give treatment advice
cards.body: The app is not designed to recommend medications, therapy plans, or prognosis. Its role is limited to screening, explanation, and helping you prepare questions for a clinician conversation.

cards.title: When a clinician discussion makes sense
cards.body: A clinician discussion can make sense when the result is elevated, when moderate results recur, when symptoms are present, or when the user wants help interpreting persistent concerns. The app should frame this as follow-up and clarification, not as confirmation of disease.

cards.title: What information is useful to bring to a visit
cards.body: Useful information includes the screening result, when the recording was made, whether you were tired or unwell, whether the room was noisy, whether the phone was held consistently, and whether you have noticed any recent voice, speech, swallowing, or movement changes.

cards.title: How to describe the app result clearly
cards.body: A balanced way to describe it is: I used a voice screening app and got a result that I would like help understanding. The app is not a diagnosis, but I want to know whether this voice pattern or any symptoms I have should be evaluated further.
